article_title: This Home Assistant integration can automate your Proxmox server
core_thesis: The Proxmox VE integration for Home Assistant (via HACS) transforms a smart home hub into a powerful homelab management tool, enabling monitoring of Proxmox nodes, LXCs, and VMs, as well as trigger-action automations that can start, stop, or suspend virtual guests based on sensor states or even external smart devices like a UPS.
detailed_summary: The article introduces the Proxmox VE integration by developer dougiteixeira, available through the Home Assistant Community Store (HACS). It allows Home Assistant to pull detailed statistics from Proxmox nodes—including CPU, memory, disk usage, and operational status for the host, containers, and VMs—and display them on customizable dashboards. Users can add clickable interactions to open service UIs or Proxmox summary pages directly from dashboard cards. The integration treats each virtual guest and node as a device entity, enabling automations where sensor states (e.g., a VM booting up) trigger actions on other guests (e.g., starting a dependent VM). The author provides examples: automatically starting a Linux Mint VM when a Pop_OS! VM boots, starting an Ollama LXC when an LLM-using container starts, and shutting down the Proxmox node when a connected UPS battery drops below 40%. Installation is straightforward via HACS, requiring the Proxmox node URL, port (default 8006), and credentials (token optional). By default, control actions (start, stop, suspend) are disabled and must be enabled per device in the Devices section. The integration is particularly valuable for multi-node clusters, allowing separate dashboards per node.

entities.name: dougiteixeira
entities.role: Developer of the Proxmox VE integration for Home Assistant

entities.name: Home Assistant Community Store (HACS)
entities.role: Community-driven store for Home Assistant integrations and add-ons

entities.name: Proxmox VE
entities.role: Open-source virtualization platform for managing VMs and containers

key_data_points: The Proxmox VE integration is installed via HACS and configured through Home Assistant's Devices & Services tab, requiring the Proxmox node URL, port (typically 8006), and user credentials. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: quick setup for monitoring and automation without complex scripting.)

The integration exposes resource consumption metrics (CPU, memory, disk) and operational status for the host, LXCs, and VMs as Home Assistant entities. (🟢 Useful for Red Teaming: monitor resource usage to detect anomalies or plan resource exhaustion attacks.)

Users can create custom dashboards with clickable interactions, e.g., clicking an LXC entity opens its web UI, while holding opens the Proxmox summary page. (🟢 Boosts homelab efficiency: centralised control from a single dashboard.)

Automations can use virtual guest status sensors as triggers and perform actions (start, stop, suspend) on other guests. Example: starting a Linux Mint VM when a Pop_OS! VM boots. (🟢 Directly applicable to your homelab: automate dependent service chains.)

The integration can combine Proxmox entities with smart home devices. Example: shutting down the Proxmox node when a UPS battery drops below 40%. (🟢 Demonstrates cross-domain automation: useful for power management in a homelab.)

Control actions (start, reboot, shutdown, suspend) are disabled by default and must be enabled per device in the Devices section. (🟢 Important safety note: prevents accidental operations until explicitly allowed.)
methodology_steps: Install HACS on your Home Assistant instance if not already done.
Download the Proxmox VE integration from the HACS store (link provided in article).

Go to Settings > Devices & Services > Add Integration, search for Proxmox VE, and enter the Proxmox node URL, port (8006), and username/password. Optionally use a token, but the author reports connection issues with tokens.
Create a dedicated Proxmox user with restricted privileges to avoid accidental damage.
After integration, go to the Devices section to enable control actions (start, stop, suspend) for each VM, LXC, or node you want to automate.
Build automations: use the binary status sensor of a virtual guest as the trigger, and select another guest or node as the action device (e.g., start, stop, suspend).
Optionally create custom dashboards with clickable title cards to open service UIs or Proxmox summary pages.
conclusions: The Proxmox VE integration effectively bridges smart home automation with homelab management, allowing users to monitor and control Proxmox resources from a single Home Assistant interface. The author's examples demonstrate practical automations that reduce manual intervention, such as starting dependent VMs or shutting down nodes during power events.

The integration is easy to deploy for anyone already using HACS, and its flexibility supports both simple monitoring and complex multi-step automations. The ability to combine Proxmox entities with smart home devices (like a UPS) opens up advanced use cases for power management and disaster recovery.

For homelab enthusiasts, this integration eliminates the need for separate monitoring tools or complex Ansible/Terraform scripts for basic automation, making it a valuable addition to a Proxmox-centric lab.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, as it provides a practical method to integrate Proxmox server management into Home Assistant, enabling both monitoring and automation without scripting. You can leverage this to create automated workflows for your virtual machines and containers, such as starting dependent services or shutting down nodes during power outages. The integration also offers a centralised dashboard for resource metrics, which is useful for capacity planning and troubleshooting. As a programmer, you can extend these automations with custom sensors or actions, and the cross-domain automation (e.g., UPS + Proxmox) demonstrates a pattern you can adapt for other smart home devices in your lab.
extra_advice: Consider using this integration to automate security-related tasks: for example, trigger a VM snapshot or shutdown when an intrusion detection sensor (e.g., from Frigate or a motion sensor) is activated. You could also combine it with a VPN status sensor to automatically spin up a honeypot VM when external access is detected.
